From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Ongoing armed conflict in the Middle East

This article is about the ongoing war in the Gaza Strip and Israel. For the broader conflicts, see [Gaza–Israel conflict](/wiki/Gaza%E2%80%93Israel_conflict "Gaza–Israel conflict") and [Israeli–Palestinian conflict](/wiki/Israeli%E2%80%93Palestinian_conflict "Israeli–Palestinian conflict"). For previous wars, see [Gaza war (disambiguation)](/wiki/Gaza_war_(disambiguation) "Gaza war (disambiguation)").

The **Gaza war**[[h]](#cite_note-31) is an armed conflict in the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip") and [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"), fought as part of the unresolved [Israeli–Palestinian](/wiki/Israeli%E2%80%93Palestinian_conflict "Israeli–Palestinian conflict") and [Gaza–Israel](/wiki/Gaza%E2%80%93Israel_conflict "Gaza–Israel conflict") conflicts. The war began on 7 October 2023, when the Palestinian militant group [Hamas](/wiki/Hamas "Hamas") led [a surprise attack on Israel](/wiki/October_7_attacks "October 7 attacks"), in which 1,195 [Israelis](/wiki/Israelis "Israelis") and foreign nationals were killed and [251 were taken hostage](/wiki/Gaza_war_hostage_crisis "Gaza war hostage crisis"). Since the start of the Israeli offensive that followed, over 72,000 [Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinians "Palestinians") in Gaza [have been killed](/wiki/Casualties_of_the_Gaza_war "Casualties of the Gaza war").[[11]](#cite_note-deaths-14)[[24]](#cite_note-32) Several studies in *[The Lancet](/wiki/The_Lancet "The Lancet")* indicate this figure is likely a significant undercount.[[25]](#cite_note-Lancet2025-33)[[26]](#cite_note-34)[[27]](#cite_note-35)[[28]](#cite_note-36) A wide consensus of scholarship has concluded that Israel is committing [genocide in Gaza](/wiki/Genocide_in_Gaza "Genocide in Gaza").[[i]](#cite_note-42) Israel and several other countries, including the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), [reject](/wiki/Gaza_genocide_denial "Gaza genocide denial") that conclusion.[[34]](#cite_note-43)[[35]](#cite_note-44)[[36]](#cite_note-45)

Following the October 7 attacks, Israel launched a [bombing campaign](/wiki/Israeli_bombing_of_the_Gaza_Strip "Israeli bombing of the Gaza Strip") and later invaded Gaza on 27 October after clearing militants from its territory. [Israeli forces](/wiki/Israeli_forces "Israeli forces") launched several campaigns, including the [Rafah offensive](/wiki/Rafah_offensive "Rafah offensive"), three battles fought around [Khan Yunis](/wiki/Khan_Yunis "Khan Yunis"), and the [siege of North Gaza](/wiki/Siege_of_North_Gaza "Siege of North Gaza"), culminating in a 2025 [offensive in Gaza City](/wiki/2025_Gaza_City_offensive "2025 Gaza City offensive"). They have assassinated Hamas leaders inside and outside of Gaza. A [2023 ceasefire](/wiki/2023_Gaza_war_ceasefire "2023 Gaza war ceasefire") broke down, and a [second ceasefire in January 2025](/wiki/January_2025_Gaza_war_ceasefire "January 2025 Gaza war ceasefire") ended with [a surprise attack by Israel](/wiki/March_2025_Israeli_attacks_on_the_Gaza_Strip "March 2025 Israeli attacks on the Gaza Strip") in March. A third ceasefire came into effect on 10 October after Israel and Hamas agreed to phase one of a [US-backed peace plan](/wiki/Gaza_peace_plan "Gaza peace plan").[[37]](#cite_note-theguardian20251009-46)[[38]](#cite_note-wsj20251010-47)

The war has resulted in a [humanitarian crisis in Gaza](/wiki/Gaza_humanitarian_crisis "Gaza humanitarian crisis"). [Israel's tightened blockade](/wiki/Israeli_blockade_of_the_Gaza_Strip_(2023%E2%80%93present) "Israeli blockade of the Gaza Strip (2023–present)") cut off basic necessities, [causing a severe hunger crisis and partial famine](/wiki/Gaza_Strip_famine "Gaza Strip famine").[[39]](#cite_note-48)[[40]](#cite_note-RIFGS-49)[[41]](#cite_note-50)[[42]](#cite_note-51) Around 90% of Gaza's civilian infrastructure has been destroyed and essential services, including water, electricity, and sanitation, have been severely disrupted;[[43]](#cite_note-52) large parts are uninhabitable,[[44]](#cite_note-amnesty20241205-53) with most hospitals, [religious and cultural landmarks](/wiki/Destruction_of_cultural_heritage_during_the_Israeli_invasion_of_the_Gaza_Strip "Destruction of cultural heritage during the Israeli invasion of the Gaza Strip"),[[45]](#cite_note-Ahmed-2024-54) and [educational facilities](/wiki/Attacks_on_schools_during_the_Israeli_invasion_of_Gaza "Attacks on schools during the Israeli invasion of Gaza") destroyed.[[46]](#cite_note-55)[[47]](#cite_note-56) Gazan [journalists](/wiki/Killing_of_journalists_in_the_Gaza_war "Killing of journalists in the Gaza war"),[[48]](#cite_note-57) [health workers](/wiki/Killing_of_health_workers_in_the_Gaza_war "Killing of health workers in the Gaza war"),[[49]](#cite_note-Human_Rights_Watch-2024-58) aid workers and other members of civil society have been detained, tortured,[[50]](#cite_note-thenewarab20241114-59)[[51]](#cite_note-TheGuardian2802025-60) and killed.[[52]](#cite_note-61) Israel has [detained](/wiki/Mass_detentions_in_the_Gaza_war "Mass detentions in the Gaza war") thousands of Palestinians from Gaza and the West Bank since the start of the war.[[53]](#cite_note-62) Nearly all of the strip's 2.3 million Palestinians have been [forcibly displaced](/wiki/Forcibly_displaced "Forcibly displaced").[[54]](#cite_note-63)[[55]](#cite_note-64) Over 100,000 Israelis were internally displaced at the height of the conflict.[[56]](#cite_note-65) The first day was the deadliest in Israel's history, and the war is the deadliest for Palestinians in the broader conflict.[[57]](#cite_note-66)

In September 2025, the [UN Commission of Inquiry on the Occupied Palestinian Territory](/wiki/Independent_International_Commission_of_Inquiry_on_the_Occupied_Palestinian_Territory "Independent International Commission of Inquiry on the Occupied Palestinian Territory") concluded that four of the five [acts of genocide](/wiki/Genocide_Convention#Definition_of_genocide "Genocide Convention") as defined by the Genocide Convention have been committed in Gaza since 7 October 2023.[[58]](#cite_note-67) [A case](/wiki/South_Africa%27s_genocide_case_against_Israel "South Africa's genocide case against Israel") accusing Israel of genocide is being reviewed by the [International Court of Justice](/wiki/International_Court_of_Justice "International Court of Justice").[[59]](#cite_note-Al_Jazeera-2024-68) Experts and human rights organizations have also stated that Israel and Hamas [have committed other war crimes](/wiki/War_crimes_in_the_Gaza_war "War crimes in the Gaza war"). Torture and [sexual violence](/wiki/Sexual_violence "Sexual violence") have been committed both [by Palestinian militant groups](/wiki/Sexual_and_gender-based_violence_in_the_October_7_attacks "Sexual and gender-based violence in the October 7 attacks") and [by Israeli forces](/wiki/Sexual_and_gender-based_violence_against_Palestinians_during_the_Gaza_war "Sexual and gender-based violence against Palestinians during the Gaza war").[[60]](#cite_note-69)

Israel has received extensive military and diplomatic [support from the United States](/wiki/United_States_support_for_Israel_in_the_Gaza_war "United States support for Israel in the Gaza war").[[61]](#cite_note-70) The war has [reverberated regionally](/wiki/Middle_Eastern_crisis_(2023%E2%80%93present) "Middle Eastern crisis (2023–present)"), with "[Axis of Resistance](/wiki/Axis_of_Resistance "Axis of Resistance")" groups across several Arab countries and [Iran clashing with the US](/wiki/Attacks_on_US_bases_during_the_Gaza_war "Attacks on US bases during the Gaza war") and Israel, including the [Twelve-Day War](/wiki/Twelve-Day_War "Twelve-Day War") in 2025, and the [2026 Iran war](/wiki/2026_Iran_war "2026 Iran war").[[62]](#cite_note-nytimes20260228-71) [A year of strikes](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93Hezbollah_conflict_(2023%E2%80%93present) "Israel–Hezbollah conflict (2023–present)") between Israel and [Hezbollah](/wiki/Hezbollah "Hezbollah") led to the [Israeli invasion of Lebanon](/wiki/2024_Israeli_invasion_of_Lebanon "2024 Israeli invasion of Lebanon") and ongoing [Israeli operations in Syria](/wiki/Israeli_invasion_of_Syria_(2024%E2%80%93present) "Israeli invasion of Syria (2024–present)"), which contributed to the [fall of the Assad regime](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Assad_regime "Fall of the Assad regime"). The war continues to have regional and international repercussions, with [large protests](/wiki/Gaza_war_protests "Gaza war protests") worldwide as well as a surge of [antisemitism](/wiki/Antisemitism_during_the_Gaza_war "Antisemitism during the Gaza war") and [anti-Palestinian racism](/wiki/Anti-Palestinianism_during_the_Gaza_war "Anti-Palestinianism during the Gaza war").

## Names

Palestinian militant groups refer to the conflict as the "battle of al-Aqsa Flood" ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): معركة طوفان الأقصى), in reference to [Operation al-Aqsa Flood](/wiki/Operation_al-Aqsa_Flood "Operation al-Aqsa Flood").[[63]](#cite_note-72)[[64]](#cite_note-73) The [government of Israel](/wiki/Thirty-seventh_government_of_Israel "Thirty-seventh government of Israel") referred to it as the "Iron Swords war" ([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): מלחמת חרבות ברזל)[[65]](#cite_note-74)[[j]](#cite_note-77) until October 2025, when it began using "War of Redemption".[[68]](#cite_note-78)[[69]](#cite_note-79) Within Israel, it is popularly referred to as the "October 7 war".[[70]](#cite_note-80) It has also been referred to as the "Simchat Torah war" because Hamas's attack began on the Jewish holiday of [Simchat Torah](/wiki/Simchat_Torah "Simchat Torah").[[71]](#cite_note-81) Media outlets have variably described it as the "Israel–Hamas war",[[72]](#cite_note-82) "war on Gaza",[[73]](#cite_note-83) "October 7 war",[[74]](#cite_note-84)[[75]](#cite_note-85) and the "second [Nakba](/wiki/Nakba "Nakba")".[[76]](#cite_note-86)[[77]](#cite_note-87)

## Background

Main article: [Background to the Gaza war](/wiki/Background_to_the_Gaza_war "Background to the Gaza war")

[![A bar chart from 2008 to before October 2023. 6,407 Palestinians have been killed during this time frame, while a smaller 308 Israelis have been killed.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Timeline_of_Israel-Palestine_fatalities_2008-2023.png/330px-Timeline_of_Israel-Palestine_fatalities_2008-2023.png)](/wiki/File:Timeline_of_Israel-Palestine_fatalities_2008-2023.png)

[Israeli](/wiki/Israeli_casualties_of_war "Israeli casualties of war") and [Palestinian deaths](/wiki/Palestinian_casualties_of_war "Palestinian casualties of war") preceding the 2023 Hamas-led attack on Israel. Most of these deaths were civilians.[[78]](#cite_note-OCHAoPt-88)[[79]](#cite_note-Alfonseca-2023-89)

The Gaza war is part of the [Israeli–Palestinian conflict](/wiki/Israeli%E2%80%93Palestinian_conflict "Israeli–Palestinian conflict").[[80]](#cite_note-90)[[81]](#cite_note-91) In the [1948 Palestine war](/wiki/1948_Palestine_war "1948 Palestine war"), [over 700,000 Palestinians fled or were expelled](/wiki/1948_Palestinian_expulsion_and_flight "1948 Palestinian expulsion and flight") and the [State of Israel](/wiki/State_of_Israel "State of Israel") was [established](/wiki/Israeli_Declaration_of_Independence "Israeli Declaration of Independence") over most of what had been [Mandatory Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine"), with the exception of two separated territories that became known as the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank") and the Gaza Strip.[[82]](#cite_note-92) Following the 1967 [Six-Day War](/wiki/Six-Day_War "Six-Day War"), Israel occupied both [Palestinian territories](/wiki/Palestinian_territories "Palestinian territories").[[83]](#cite_note-El_Deeb-2023-93) The upcoming period witnessed two popular uprisings by [Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinians "Palestinians") against the [Israeli occupation](/wiki/Israeli_occupation "Israeli occupation"): the [First](/wiki/First_Intifada "First Intifada") and [Second Intifadas](/wiki/Second_Intifada "Second Intifada") in 1987 and 2000,[[84]](#cite_note-94) with the latter's end seeing [Israel's unilateral withdrawal from Gaza](/wiki/Israeli_disengagement_from_Gaza "Israeli disengagement from Gaza") in 2005.[[85]](#cite_note-95)[[86]](#cite_note-96)

Since 2007, the Gaza Strip has been governed by [Hamas](/wiki/Hamas "Hamas"), an [Islamist](/wiki/Islamist "Islamist") militant group, while the West Bank remained under the control of the [Palestinian Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_Authority "Palestinian Authority"). After Hamas's takeover, Israel imposed a [blockade](/wiki/Blockade_of_the_Gaza_Strip "Blockade of the Gaza Strip")[[87]](#cite_note-97)[[88]](#cite_note-Kane-2023-98) that significantly damaged [Gaza's economy](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Gaza_Strip "Economy of the Gaza Strip").[[89]](#cite_note-WBEUUN-99) Israel justified the blockade by citing security concerns,[[90]](#cite_note-Meakem-2023-100) but international rights groups have characterized it as [collective punishment](/wiki/Collective_punishment "Collective punishment").[[91]](#cite_note-101)[[92]](#cite_note-102)[[93]](#cite_note-Abdulrahim-2023-103) By 2023, [UNRWA](/wiki/UNRWA "UNRWA") reported that 81% of people were living below the [poverty level](/wiki/Poverty_level "Poverty level"), with 63% being food insecure and dependent on international assistance.[[79]](#cite_note-Alfonseca-2023-89)[[94]](#cite_note-104)

Since 2007, Israel and Hamas, along with other Palestinian militant groups based in Gaza, have engaged in conflict,[[90]](#cite_note-Meakem-2023-100)[[88]](#cite_note-Kane-2023-98)[[95]](#cite_note-Beauchamp-2023-105) including four wars, in [2008–2009](/wiki/Gaza_War_(2008%E2%80%932009) "Gaza War (2008–2009)"), [2012](/wiki/2012_Gaza_War "2012 Gaza War"), [2014](/wiki/2014_Gaza_War "2014 Gaza War"), and [2021](/wiki/2021_Israel%E2%80%93Palestine_crisis "2021 Israel–Palestine crisis").[[96]](#cite_note-106)[[97]](#cite_note-107)[[98]](#cite_note-108) Combined, these conflicts killed approximately [6,400 Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinian_casualties_of_war "Palestinian casualties of war") and [300 Israelis](/wiki/Israeli_casualties_of_war "Israeli casualties of war").[[99]](#cite_note-109)[[78]](#cite_note-OCHAoPt-88)[[79]](#cite_note-Alfonseca-2023-89) In 2018–2019, there were large [organized protests near the Gaza-Israel border](/wiki/2018%E2%80%932019_Gaza_border_protests "2018–2019 Gaza border protests") to call for the [right to return](/wiki/Palestinian_right_of_return "Palestinian right of return"). The [Israel Defense Forces](/wiki/Israel_Defense_Forces "Israel Defense Forces") (IDF) violently suppressed the protests, killing hundreds and injuring thousands of Palestinians by sniper fire.[[100]](#cite_note-110)[[101]](#cite_note-111) Soon after [a short 2021 conflict](/wiki/2021_Israel%E2%80%93Palestine_crisis "2021 Israel–Palestine crisis"), Hamas's military wing, the [Al-Qassam Brigades](/wiki/Al-Qassam_Brigades "Al-Qassam Brigades"), started planning an operation against Israel, which became the 7 October attacks.[[102]](#cite_note-Klein-2023-112)[[103]](#cite_note-113) According to diplomats, Hamas had repeatedly said in the months leading up to October 2023 that it did not want another military escalation in Gaza as it would worsen the humanitarian crisis.[[93]](#cite_note-Abdulrahim-2023-103)

Hamas officials stated that the attack was a response to the [Israeli occupation](/wiki/Israeli_occupation "Israeli occupation"), [blockade of the Gaza Strip](/wiki/Blockade_of_the_Gaza_Strip "Blockade of the Gaza Strip"), desecration of the [Al-Aqsa Mosque](/wiki/Al-Aqsa_Mosque "Al-Aqsa Mosque"), [Israeli settler violence](/wiki/Israeli_settler_violence "Israeli settler violence") against Palestinians, [restrictions on the movement of Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinian_freedom_of_movement "Palestinian freedom of movement"), and [imprisonment of thousands of Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinian_prisoners_in_Israel "Palestinian prisoners in Israel"), whom Hamas sought to release by taking Israeli hostages.[[104]](#cite_note-aj7oct-invasion-114)[[105]](#cite_note-McKernan-20231014-115)[[106]](#cite_note-Pacchiani-2023-116) Numerous commentators have identified the broader context of Israeli occupation as a cause of the war.[[107]](#cite_note-Matar-2023-117)[[108]](#cite_note-118)[[109]](#cite_note-119) Several human rights organizations, including [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International"),[[110]](#cite_note-120) [B'Tselem](/wiki/B%27Tselem "B'Tselem")[[111]](#cite_note-121) and [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch")[[112]](#cite_note-122) have [likened the Israeli occupation to apartheid](/wiki/Israel_and_apartheid "Israel and apartheid"), although supporters of Israel dispute this characterization.[[113]](#cite_note-123)[[114]](#cite_note-124) The [Netanyahu](/wiki/Netanyahu "Netanyahu") government has been criticized within Israel for granting [work permits to Gazan residents](/wiki/Israeli_permit_regime_in_the_Gaza_Strip "Israeli permit regime in the Gaza Strip"), facilitating the transfer of funds to Hamas and pursuing relative calm. These actions have been criticized as having backfired in light of the attacks on 7 October 2023.[[115]](#cite_note-Schneider-2023-125)[[116]](#cite_note-Foreign_Policy-2021-126)[[117]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times-2023-127)

At the time of the attack, Israel and Saudi Arabia were conducting negotiations to [normalize relations](/wiki/Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_normalization "Arab–Israeli normalization").[[118]](#cite_note-128) Hamas leaders such as [Ismail Haniyeh](/wiki/Ismail_Haniyeh "Ismail Haniyeh") cited disrupting this "normalization train" as a motive for the October 7 attacks.[[119]](#cite_note-129)

## War in Israel and Gaza

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of the Gaza war](/wiki/Timeline_of_the_Gaza_war "Timeline of the Gaza war").

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of military engagements during the Gaza war](/wiki/List_of_military_engagements_during_the_Gaza_war "List of military engagements during the Gaza war").

### 7 October Hamas-led attack on Israel

On the morning of 7 October 2023,[[120]](#cite_note-aj7oct-retaliation-130) during the Jewish holidays of Simchat Torah and [Shemini Atzeret](/wiki/Shemini_Atzeret "Shemini Atzeret") on [Shabbat](/wiki/Shabbat "Shabbat"),[[121]](#cite_note-131) Hamas announced the start of "[Operation Al-Aqsa Flood](/wiki/Operation_Al-Aqsa_Flood "Operation Al-Aqsa Flood")", firing between 3,000 and 5,000 rockets from the Gaza Strip into Israel within a span of 20 minutes, killing at least five people.[[122]](#cite_note-132)[[123]](#cite_note-aj7oct-escalation-133)[[124]](#cite_note-McKernan-20231007-134) In the evening, Hamas launched another barrage of 150 rockets.[[123]](#cite_note-aj7oct-escalation-133) Simultaneously, around 3,000 Hamas militants[[17]](#cite_note-strength1-22) infiltrated Israel from Gaza using trucks, motorcycles, bulldozers, speedboats, and paragliders.[[125]](#cite_note-Federman-20231007-135)[[120]](#cite_note-aj7oct-retaliation-130)[[126]](#cite_note-Kingsley-2023-136) They took over checkpoints at [Kerem Shalom](/wiki/Kerem_Shalom_border_crossing "Kerem Shalom border crossing") and [Erez](/wiki/Erez_Crossing "Erez Crossing"), and created openings in the border fence in five other places.[[127]](#cite_note-137)

Militants massacred civilians in several [kibbutzim](/wiki/Kibbutz "Kibbutz"),[[128]](#cite_note-138)[[129]](#cite_note-The_Economist-20231012-139) where they took hostages[[130]](#cite_note-140) and set fire to homes.[[131]](#cite_note-ynet7oct2-141) In [a massacre at an outdoor music festival near Re'im](/wiki/Nova_music_festival_massacre "Nova music festival massacre"), at least 325 people were killed, with more injured or taken hostage.[[132]](#cite_note-Zitun-2023-142)[[133]](#cite_note-143) In total, 251 people, mostly civilians, were [taken hostage](/wiki/Gaza_war_hostage_crisis "Gaza war hostage crisis"), including[[134]](#cite_note-Vinograd-2023-144)[[135]](#cite_note-TAI_1-145) children, elderly people, and soldiers.[[136]](#cite_note-146) Hamas militants also reportedly engaged in mutilation, torture, and [sexual and gender-based violence](/wiki/Sexual_and_gender-based_violence_in_the_2023_Hamas-led_attack_on_Israel "Sexual and gender-based violence in the 2023 Hamas-led attack on Israel").[[137]](#cite_note-washingtonpost20231125-147)[[138]](#cite_note-148)[[139]](#cite_note-NYT28Dec2023-149)

The attack was a complete surprise to the Israeli citizens.[[140]](#cite_note-150) The 7 October attacks were described as "an intelligence failure for the ages"[[141]](#cite_note-151) and a "[failure of imagination](/wiki/Failure_of_imagination "Failure of imagination")" on the part of the Israeli government.[[142]](#cite_note-152) It later emerged that abnormal Hamas movements had been detected the previous day by Israeli intelligence, but the military's alert level was not raised and political leaders were not informed.[[143]](#cite_note-153)

*[The Economist](/wiki/The_Economist "The Economist")* noted that "the assault dwarf[ed] all other mass murders of Israeli civilians", and that "the last time before October 7th that this many Jews were murdered on a single day was during [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust")."[[129]](#cite_note-The_Economist-20231012-139) According to both Hamas officials and external observers, the attack was a calculated effort to create a "permanent" state of war and revive the Palestinian cause.[[144]](#cite_note-gambit-154)[[145]](#cite_note-155)

The IDF began Israel's counter-attack several hours after the Hamas-led invasion.[[146]](#cite_note-156) The first helicopters sent to support the military reached the Israeli areas surrounding the Gaza Strip an hour after the fighting began.[[132]](#cite_note-Zitun-2023-142) Their crews encountered difficulties in determining which places were occupied by invading militants, and distinguishing between Israeli civilians, IDF soldiers, and Palestinian militants on the ground.[[132]](#cite_note-Zitun-2023-142) A June 2024 UN report[[147]](#cite_note-157)[[148]](#cite_note-158) and a July 2024 *Haaretz* investigation revealed that the IDF ordered the [Hannibal Directive](/wiki/Hannibal_Directive "Hannibal Directive") to be used, killing an unknown number of Israeli civilians and soldiers.[[149]](#cite_note-Yaniv-2024-159)[[150]](#cite_note-AJ-2024-07-09-160)[[151]](#cite_note-ABC_News-2024-161)

In a televised broadcast, [Benjamin Netanyahu](/wiki/Benjamin_Netanyahu "Benjamin Netanyahu"), [Prime Minister of Israel](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Israel "Prime Minister of Israel"), announced that the country was at war.[[126]](#cite_note-Kingsley-2023-136) He threatened to "turn all the places where Hamas is organized and hiding into cities of ruins", called Gaza "the city of evil", and urged its residents to leave.[[152]](#cite_note-162)[[104]](#cite_note-aj7oct-invasion-114) Overnight, Israel's [Security Cabinet](/wiki/Security_Cabinet_of_Israel "Security Cabinet of Israel") voted to act to bring about the "destruction of the military and governmental capabilities of [Hamas](/wiki/Hamas "Hamas") and [Palestinian Islamic Jihad](/wiki/Palestinian_Islamic_Jihad "Palestinian Islamic Jihad")".[[153]](#cite_note-163) The [Israel Electric Corporation](/wiki/Israel_Electric_Corporation "Israel Electric Corporation"), which supplies 80% of Gaza's electricity, cut off power to the area.[[154]](#cite_note-Gritten-2023-164) The IDF declared a "state of readiness for war",[[123]](#cite_note-aj7oct-escalation-133) mobilized tens of thousands of army [reservists](/wiki/Reservist "Reservist"),[[120]](#cite_note-aj7oct-retaliation-130)[[154]](#cite_note-Gritten-2023-164) and declared a state of emergency for areas within 80 kilometers (50 mi) of Gaza.[[155]](#cite_note-Bayer-2023-165) While [Ben Gurion Airport](/wiki/Ben_Gurion_Airport "Ben Gurion Airport") and [Ramon Airport](/wiki/Ramon_Airport "Ramon Airport") remained operational, multiple airlines cancelled flights.[[156]](#cite_note-166) On 9 or 10 October, Hamas offered to release all civilian hostages held in Gaza if Israel would call off its planned invasion of the Gaza Strip, but the Israeli government rejected the offer.[[157]](#cite_note-167)

Following the surprise attack, the [Israeli Air Force](/wiki/Israeli_Air_Force "Israeli Air Force") conducted airstrikes that they said targeted Hamas targets,[[123]](#cite_note-aj7oct-escalation-133)[[155]](#cite_note-Bayer-2023-165)[[158]](#cite_note-Knell-2023-168) employing its [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence") *Habsora* ("The Gospel") software.[[159]](#cite_note-169)[[160]](#cite_note-Abraham-2023-170) These airstrikes killed, on average, 350 persons per day during the first 20 days.[[161]](#cite_note-171) Israel rescued two hostages before declaring a [state of war](/wiki/State_of_war "State of war") for the first time since the [1973 Yom Kippur War](/wiki/1973_Yom_Kippur_War "1973 Yom Kippur War").[[162]](#cite_note-172)[[163]](#cite_note-173) On 9 October, Defense Minister Gallant announced a "complete siege" of the Gaza Strip, cutting off electricity and blocking the entry of food and fuel.[[164]](#cite_note-174) Gallant backed down under pressure from US President [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden"), and a deal was struck ten days later to allow aid into Gaza.[[165]](#cite_note-Fergal_Keane-2024-175) The first such aid convoy entered Gaza on 21 October,[[166]](#cite_note-176) while fuel did not arrive until November.[[167]](#cite_note-177)

On 13 October, the [IDF](/wiki/IDF "IDF") ordered all civilians in [Gaza City](/wiki/Gaza_City "Gaza City") to evacuate to areas south of the [Wadi Gaza](/wiki/Wadi_Gaza "Wadi Gaza")[[168]](#cite_note-178) within 24 hours. The Hamas Authority for Refugee Affairs told residents in northern Gaza to defy those orders.[[169]](#cite_note-179) The Israeli order was widely condemned as "outrageous" and "impossible", and calls were made for it to be reversed.[[170]](#cite_note-180) As a part of the order, the IDF outlined a six-hour window on 13 October for refugees to flee south along specified routes.[[171]](#cite_note-181) [An explosion](/wiki/Attacks_on_Palestinians_evacuating_Gaza_City#13_October_2023 "Attacks on Palestinians evacuating Gaza City") along one of the safe routes killed 70 Palestinians. Israel and Hamas blamed each other for the attack.[[172]](#cite_note-182) The IDF said Hamas set up roadblocks to keep Gaza residents from evacuating.[[173]](#cite_note-183) Israeli officials, foreign governments and intergovernmental organizations condemned Hamas's alleged use of [hospitals and civilians as human shields](/wiki/Use_of_human_shields_by_Hamas "Use of human shields by Hamas"), which Hamas contested.[[174]](#cite_note-184)[[175]](#cite_note-185)

On 17 October, Israel bombed areas of southern Gaza.[[176]](#cite_note-186) Late in the evening, an [explosion occurred](/wiki/Al-Ahli_Arab_Hospital_explosion "Al-Ahli Arab Hospital explosion") in the parking lot of the [Al-Ahli Arabi Baptist Hospital](/wiki/Al-Ahli_Arabi_Baptist_Hospital "Al-Ahli Arabi Baptist Hospital") in the center of Gaza City, killing hundreds. The ongoing conflict prevented independent on-site analysis.[[177]](#cite_note-187) Palestinian statements that it was an Israeli airstrike were denied by the IDF, which stated that the explosion resulted from a failed rocket launch by [Palestinian Islamic Jihad](/wiki/Palestinian_Islamic_Jihad "Palestinian Islamic Jihad"),[[178]](#cite_note-188) who denied any involvement.[[179]](#cite_note-189)

Israeli military during ground operations in Gaza

On 27 October, after building up an invasion force of over 100,000 soldiers, the IDF launched a large-scale ground incursion into parts of northern Gaza.[[180]](#cite_note-190)[[181]](#cite_note-191) Israeli airstrikes targeted the area around [al-Quds hospital](/wiki/Al-Quds_hospital "Al-Quds hospital"),[[182]](#cite_note-Jackson-2023-192) where around 14,000 civilians were believed to be sheltering.[[182]](#cite_note-Jackson-2023-192) The following day, the IDF [struck Jabalia refugee camp](/wiki/31_October_2023_Jabalia_refugee_camp_airstrike "31 October 2023 Jabalia refugee camp airstrike"), killing 50 and wounding 150 Palestinians. Israel said the attack killed a senior Hamas commander, whose presence Hamas denied, and dozens of militants.[[183]](#cite_note-193)[[184]](#cite_note-194)[[185]](#cite_note-195) The attack resulted in several ambassador recalls.[[186]](#cite_note-196)[[187]](#cite_note-Ramos-Bolivia-197)[[188]](#cite_note-198)
