Main article: [Gaza genocide](/wiki/Gaza_genocide "Gaza genocide")

See also: [Allegations of genocide in the October 7 attacks](/wiki/Allegations_of_genocide_in_the_October_7_attacks "Allegations of genocide in the October 7 attacks")

In December 2023, the government of [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") instituted proceedings, *[South Africa v. Israel](/wiki/South_Africa_v._Israel "South Africa v. Israel")*, against Israel at the [International Court of Justice](/wiki/International_Court_of_Justice "International Court of Justice") (ICJ), alleging a violation of the [Genocide Convention](/wiki/Genocide_Convention "Genocide Convention").[[520]](#cite_note-ICJ-2023-12-29-533) In January 2024, the court ordered Israel to take all measures within its power to prevent the commission of acts of genocide, to prevent and punish [incitement to genocide](/wiki/Incitement_to_genocide "Incitement to genocide"), and to allow basic humanitarian service, aid and supplies into Gaza.[[521]](#cite_note-ICJruling-534)[[522]](#cite_note-535)[[523]](#cite_note-536) Israel did not fully comply with the court's orders.[[524]](#cite_note-Amnesty_International_Blockade-537)[[525]](#cite_note-Human_Rights_Watch_Blockade-538)[[526]](#cite_note-539)

In December 2024, [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International") concluded that Israel was committing [genocide](/wiki/Genocide "Genocide") against Palestinians in the Gaza Strip;[[44]](#cite_note-amnesty20241205-53)[[527]](#cite_note-540) [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch") also concluded that month that Israel is responsible for the crime of genocide through deprivation of safe water and sanitation.[[528]](#cite_note-541) In March 2025, the UN Commission found that Israel's attacks on women's healthcare facilities in Gaza amounted to genocidal acts;[[529]](#cite_note-542) Israeli prime minister Benjamin Netanyahu condemned the report as "false and absurd", accusing the UN Human Rights Council of being anti-Israel and anti-Semitic.[[530]](#cite_note-543) In July 2025, Israeli human rights groups [B'Tselem](/wiki/B%27Tselem "B'Tselem")[[531]](#cite_note-B'Tselem_2025-544) and [Physicians for Human Rights–Israel](/wiki/Physicians_for_Human_Rights%E2%80%93Israel "Physicians for Human Rights–Israel")[[532]](#cite_note-PHR_Jul_2025-545) released reports calling Israel's campaign in Gaza a genocide.[[531]](#cite_note-B'Tselem_2025-544)[[533]](#cite_note-Kottasová_&_Salman-546)

On 16 September 2025, the [UN Commission of Inquiry on the Occupied Palestinian Territory](/wiki/Independent_International_Commission_of_Inquiry_on_the_Occupied_Palestinian_Territory "Independent International Commission of Inquiry on the Occupied Palestinian Territory") concluded that Israel had [committed genocide against Palestinians in Gaza](/wiki/Gaza_genocide "Gaza genocide"), and that top Israeli officials including Netanyahu, Gallant, and Herzog had incited these acts. The report said that four of the five acts of genocide have been committed, as defined under the 1948 Genocide Convention. Israel rejected the report as distorted and false, and called for the abolition of the Commission.[[534]](#cite_note-547)[[33]](#cite_note-un_coi_cnn-41)

The genocide has also been recognized by [a United Nations special committee](/wiki/Special_Committee_to_Investigate_Israeli_Practices_Affecting_the_Human_Rights_of_the_Palestinian_People "Special Committee to Investigate Israeli Practices Affecting the Human Rights of the Palestinian People")[[535]](#cite_note-UNCommitteeHeadGenocide-548) and the [International Association of Genocide Scholars](/wiki/International_Association_of_Genocide_Scholars "International Association of Genocide Scholars"),[[536]](#cite_note-IAGSReuters-549)[[537]](#cite_note-IAGSResolution-550) numerous [genocide studies](/wiki/Genocide_studies "Genocide studies") and [international law](/wiki/International_law "International law") scholars,[[538]](#cite_note-growing_agreement-551)[[30]](#cite_note-genocide_int_law_scholars-38) and other experts.[[539]](#cite_note-552)[[n]](#cite_note-553)

## Spillover

Main articles: [Middle Eastern crisis (2023–present)](/wiki/Middle_Eastern_crisis_(2023%E2%80%93present) "Middle Eastern crisis (2023–present)") and [Spillover of the Gaza war in Syria](/wiki/Spillover_of_the_Gaza_war_in_Syria "Spillover of the Gaza war in Syria")

See also: [Israel–Hezbollah conflict (2023–present)](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93Hezbollah_conflict_(2023%E2%80%93present) "Israel–Hezbollah conflict (2023–present)"), [Red Sea crisis](/wiki/Red_Sea_crisis "Red Sea crisis"), [2024 Iran–Israel conflict](/wiki/2024_Iran%E2%80%93Israel_conflict "2024 Iran–Israel conflict"), [Israeli invasion of Syria (2024–present)](/wiki/Israeli_invasion_of_Syria_(2024%E2%80%93present) "Israeli invasion of Syria (2024–present)"), [Iran–Israel war](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Israel_war "Iran–Israel war"), and [Israeli attack on Doha](/wiki/Israeli_attack_on_Doha "Israeli attack on Doha")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Hezbollah_Headquarter_Airstrike_2024.jpg/330px-Hezbollah_Headquarter_Airstrike_2024.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hezbollah_Headquarter_Airstrike_2024.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Kiryat_Shmona_destruction.jpg/250px-Kiryat_Shmona_destruction.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kiryat_Shmona_destruction.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/The_USS_Dwight_D._Eisenhower_and_USS_USS_Gerald_R._Ford_carrier_strike_groups_in_November_2023.jpg/250px-The_USS_Dwight_D._Eisenhower_and_USS_USS_Gerald_R._Ford_carrier_strike_groups_in_November_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_USS_Dwight_D._Eisenhower_and_USS_USS_Gerald_R._Ford_carrier_strike_groups_in_November_2023.jpg)

* Rising smoke after the [Israel strike on Hezbollah headquarters](/wiki/2024_Hezbollah_headquarters_strike "2024 Hezbollah headquarters strike")
* A damaged building in Kiryat Shmona, northern Israel
* Two United States [carrier strike groups](/wiki/Carrier_strike_group "Carrier strike group") in the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea").

The war's spillover resulted in a [major escalation](/wiki/2024_Iran%E2%80%93Israel_conflict "2024 Iran–Israel conflict") of [existing tensions](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Israel_proxy_conflict "Iran–Israel proxy conflict") between Israel and Iran, with groups in the [Axis of Resistance](/wiki/Axis_of_Resistance "Axis of Resistance") launching [attacks on American military bases](/wiki/Attacks_on_U.S._bases_in_Iraq,_Jordan,_and_Syria_during_the_Israel%E2%80%93Hamas_war "Attacks on U.S. bases in Iraq, Jordan, and Syria during the Israel–Hamas war"), and the Yemeni [Houthi movement](/wiki/Houthi_movement "Houthi movement") [attacking](/wiki/Red_Sea_crisis "Red Sea crisis") commercial vessels in the [Red Sea](/wiki/Red_Sea "Red Sea") and incurring a US-led [military operation](/wiki/Operation_Prosperity_Guardian "Operation Prosperity Guardian").[[540]](#cite_note-554) Hezbollah in southern Lebanon and the Houthi movement launched attacks against Israel shortly after the start of the war. Iranian-backed militias in Iraq and Syria have also traded attacks with the US and IDF.[[541]](#cite_note-555)

Israel has bombed targets in and around [Damascus](/wiki/Damascus "Damascus") throughout the war,[[542]](#cite_note-Reuters-2023-556)[[543]](#cite_note-557)[[544]](#cite_note-AP_News-2024-558) with an [attack on the Iranian embassy](/wiki/Israeli_airstrike_on_the_Iranian_consulate_in_Damascus "Israeli airstrike on the Iranian consulate in Damascus") in Damascus on 1 April 2024 leading to a series of [retaliatory airstrikes](/wiki/April_2024_Iranian_strikes_against_Israel "April 2024 Iranian strikes against Israel") on Israel.[[545]](#cite_note-Tanyos-2024-559)[[546]](#cite_note-CNN-2024-560) On 31 July, Hamas political leader Ismail Haniyeh [was assassinated](/wiki/Assassination_of_Ismail_Haniyeh "Assassination of Ismail Haniyeh") in [Tehran](/wiki/Tehran "Tehran"),[[547]](#cite_note-561) and on 1 October, [Iran fired approximately 200 missiles](/wiki/October_2024_Iranian_strikes_against_Israel "October 2024 Iranian strikes against Israel") at Israel.[[548]](#cite_note-theguardian20241001-562)[[549]](#cite_note-Schneider-2024-563)

By the end of 2024, a year-long [exchange of strikes](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93Hezbollah_conflict_(2023%E2%80%93present) "Israel–Hezbollah conflict (2023–present)") between Israel and Hezbollah escalated into an [Israeli invasion of Lebanon](/wiki/2024_Israeli_invasion_of_Lebanon "2024 Israeli invasion of Lebanon"), before it was paused after a [ceasefire](/wiki/2024_Israel%E2%80%93Lebanon_ceasefire_agreement "2024 Israel–Lebanon ceasefire agreement").[[550]](#cite_note-APIH-564) In February 2025, Israel largely withdrew but maintained five military outposts on highlands in Southern Lebanon, against Lebanon's wishes.[[551]](#cite_note-565)[[552]](#cite_note-566) The crisis has also seen [the fall](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Assad_regime "Fall of the Assad regime") of the [Assad regime](/wiki/Assad_regime "Assad regime") and an ongoing [Israeli invasion of Syria](/wiki/Israeli_invasion_of_Syria_(2024%E2%80%93present) "Israeli invasion of Syria (2024–present)").[[553]](#cite_note-567)[[554]](#cite_note-568) Israeli defense minister Israel Katz in April 2025 declared that Israeli forces would indefinitely remain in "security zones" that they "cleared and seized" in Gaza, Lebanon and Syria.[[555]](#cite_note-569)

On 13 June 2025, [Israel launched large-scale attacks](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Israel_war "Iran–Israel war") against targets in several areas in Iran, killing Iran's top military leadership,[[556]](#cite_note-570) including [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/wiki/Islamic_Revolutionary_Guard_Corps "Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps") (IRGC) commander [Hossein Salami](/wiki/Hossein_Salami "Hossein Salami"),[[557]](#cite_note-571) Iranian Armed Forces Chief of Staff Major General [Mohammad Bagheri](/wiki/Mohammad_Bagheri_(general) "Mohammad Bagheri (general)"),[[558]](#cite_note-572) and nuclear scientists [Fereydoon Abbasi](/wiki/Fereydoon_Abbasi "Fereydoon Abbasi") and [Mohammad Mehdi Tehranchi](/wiki/Mohammad_Mehdi_Tehranchi "Mohammad Mehdi Tehranchi") were killed in the attacks, according to Iranian state media.[[559]](#cite_note-573) The [US joined Israel in bombing Iran](/wiki/United_States_strikes_on_Iranian_nuclear_sites "United States strikes on Iranian nuclear sites") on 22 June 2025, saying to have destroyed several nuclear sites.[[560]](#cite_note-574)

On 9 September 2025, [Israel bombed the Hamas headquarters in Qatar](/wiki/Israeli_attack_on_Doha "Israeli attack on Doha"), reportedly targeting several members of the [Hamas temporary committee](/wiki/Hamas_temporary_committee "Hamas temporary committee"), including [Khalil al-Hayya](/wiki/Khalil_al-Hayya "Khalil al-Hayya"), [Khaled Mashal](/wiki/Khaled_Mashal "Khaled Mashal"), [Muhammad Ismail Darwish](/wiki/Muhammad_Ismail_Darwish "Muhammad Ismail Darwish"), [Mousa Abu Marzook](/wiki/Mousa_Abu_Marzook "Mousa Abu Marzook"), and [Zaher Jabarin](/wiki/Zaher_Jabarin "Zaher Jabarin").[[561]](#cite_note-NYT20250909-575)[[562]](#cite_note-TOI2025-576) The attack failed to assassinate the Hamas leadership in Qatar.[[563]](#cite_note-577) The stated Israeli reason for the bombing was the [Ramot Junction shooting](/wiki/Ramot_Junction_shooting "Ramot Junction shooting") in East Jerusalem the day prior.[[564]](#cite_note-578)

In February 2026, United States launched a [major attack on Iran](/wiki/2026_Israeli%E2%80%93United_States_strikes_on_Iran "2026 Israeli–United States strikes on Iran") with Israel with the stated goal of [regime change](/wiki/Regime_change "Regime change").[[62]](#cite_note-nytimes20260228-71)

### West Bank and Israel

Further information: [Israeli incursions in the West Bank during the Gaza war](/wiki/Israeli_incursions_in_the_West_Bank_during_the_Gaza_war "Israeli incursions in the West Bank during the Gaza war")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/2023_Israel%E2%80%93Hamas_war_-_West_Bank.svg/250px-2023_Israel%E2%80%93Hamas_war_-_West_Bank.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2023_Israel%E2%80%93Hamas_war_-_West_Bank.svg)

West Bank sector of war  

West Bank [Palestinian enclaves](/wiki/Palestinian_enclaves "Palestinian enclaves") ([Areas A & B](/wiki/West_Bank_Areas_in_the_Oslo_II_Accord "West Bank Areas in the Oslo II Accord"))

West Bank under Israeli control ([Area C](/wiki/Area_C "Area C"))

Israeli-annexed [East Jerusalem](/wiki/East_Jerusalem "East Jerusalem")

Violence in the West Bank increased from the beginning of the war, with more than 607 Palestinians and over 25 Israelis killed by August 2024.[[565]](#cite_note-579)[[566]](#cite_note-580) At the same time, [Israeli settler violence](/wiki/Israeli_settler_violence "Israeli settler violence") further increased to around 1,270 attacks, against 856 for all of 2022.[[567]](#cite_note-Knell-Luckhurst-2024-581) In the weeks after the 7 October attack, about 1,000 Palestinians were forcibly displaced by settlers and Israeli forces,[[568]](#cite_note-582) and six Palestinian communities were abandoned.[[569]](#cite_note-583) On 19 October, more than 60 Hamas members were arrested and 13 people were killed in overnight [Israeli raids](/wiki/October_2023_Tulkarm_raid "October 2023 Tulkarm raid") across the West Bank.[[570]](#cite_note-584)[[571]](#cite_note-585)

In July 2024, Israeli authorities approved the seizure of 12.7 square kilometers in the West Bank, the largest single land seizure since the 1993 Oslo accords.[[572]](#cite_note-APWB-586) Israeli authorities also approved plans for almost 5,300 new houses in occupied West Bank.[[573]](#cite_note-587) By this point, Israeli land seizures exceeded the combined total of the previous 20 years.[[574]](#cite_note-588) The following month, the Israeli government approved new settlements in the area.[[575]](#cite_note-589)[[576]](#cite_note-590)

On 7 August, [Wafa](/wiki/Wafa "Wafa") reported that Israeli forces destroyed the regional headquarters of [Fatah](/wiki/Fatah "Fatah") in the [Balata Camp](/wiki/Balata_Camp "Balata Camp").[[577]](#cite_note-591)[[578]](#cite_note-592) On 28 August, Israel launched [the largest military operation](/wiki/2024_Israeli_military_operation_in_the_northern_West_Bank "2024 Israeli military operation in the northern West Bank") into the northern West Bank in more than 20 years. Israeli Foreign Minister [Israel Katz](/wiki/Israel_Katz "Israel Katz") called the operation a "full-fledged war".[[579]](#cite_note-593) Israeli forces carried out simultaneous operations in Jenin, Tubas, Nablus, Ramallah and Tulkarm.[[580]](#cite_note-594) The IDF classified the West Bank as a "combat zone" in early September, viewing it as the second most important front in the war.[[581]](#cite_note-595)[[582]](#cite_note-596) Yoav Gallant said that Israel was "[mowing the lawn](/wiki/Mowing_the_lawn "Mowing the lawn")" with its West Bank operations, but that it would eventually need to "pull out the roots".[[583]](#cite_note-597) On 6 September, Turkish-American protestor [Ayşenur Eygi was killed by an Israeli sniper](/wiki/Killing_of_Aysenur_Eygi "Killing of Aysenur Eygi") at a demonstration near Nablus.[[584]](#cite_note-598)

On 3 October, [an Israeli airstrike](/wiki/Tulkarm_camp_airstrike "Tulkarm camp airstrike") in [Tulkarm Camp](/wiki/Tulkarm_Camp "Tulkarm Camp") killed at least 20 people.[[585]](#cite_note-599)[[586]](#cite_note-600) On 13 November, Israeli finance minister [Bezalel Smotrich](/wiki/Bezalel_Smotrich "Bezalel Smotrich") said that with Trump's victory in the [2024 United States presidential election](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election"), Israel was "a step away" from "sovereignty in Judea and Samaria," with later comments by [the future US ambassador to Israel](/wiki/Mike_Huckabee "Mike Huckabee") corroborating the possibility of annexation of the West Bank.[[587]](#cite_note-601) On 21 January 2025, the IDF launched a major [raid](/wiki/2025_Israeli_operation_in_the_West_Bank "2025 Israeli operation in the West Bank") in the West Bank.[[588]](#cite_note-602)[[589]](#cite_note-603) On 8 September, two Hamas militants [opened fire inside a bus](/wiki/2025_Ramot_Junction_shooting "2025 Ramot Junction shooting"), killing six Israelis and injuring 21 in [East Jerusalem](/wiki/East_Jerusalem "East Jerusalem").[[590]](#cite_note-604)

On 30 November 2023, two Palestinian gunmen [killed](/wiki/2023_Givat_Shaul_shooting "2023 Givat Shaul shooting") three and wounded 11 Israeli civilians at a bus stop on the [Givat Shaul](/wiki/Givat_Shaul "Givat Shaul") Interchange in Jerusalem. Hamas claimed responsibility.[[591]](#cite_note-605) On 16 February 2024, a Palestinian gunman [shot and killed](/wiki/2024_Kiryat_Malakhi_attack "2024 Kiryat Malakhi attack") two Israeli civilians and injured four others in [Kiryat Malakhi](/wiki/Kiryat_Malakhi "Kiryat Malakhi"), Israel. The shooter was killed by an off-duty IDF reservist at the scene.[[592]](#cite_note-606) On 18 August, a Hamas militant from Nablus carried out [a suicide bombing in Tel Aviv](/wiki/Lehi_Street_bombing "Lehi Street bombing"), wounding one person.[[593]](#cite_note-607) On 1 October, two Hamas gunmen from Hebron carried out [a shooting attack in Jaffa, Tel Aviv](/wiki/2024_Jaffa_shooting "2024 Jaffa shooting"), killing seven people and injuring 17 others.[[594]](#cite_note-608)

### American involvement

Further information: [United States support for Israel in the Gaza war](/wiki/United_States_support_for_Israel_in_the_Gaza_war "United States support for Israel in the Gaza war") and [2025 Donald Trump Gaza Strip takeover proposal](/wiki/2025_Donald_Trump_Gaza_Strip_takeover_proposal "2025 Donald Trump Gaza Strip takeover proposal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/U_S_Air_Force_Airmen_supply_Israel_with_missile_defense_%288086168%29.jpg/330px-U_S_Air_Force_Airmen_supply_Israel_with_missile_defense_%288086168%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:U_S_Air_Force_Airmen_supply_Israel_with_missile_defense_(8086168).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/President_Joe_Biden_meets_with_Prime_Minister_Benjamin_Netanyahu.jpg/250px-President_Joe_Biden_meets_with_Prime_Minister_Benjamin_Netanyahu.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Joe_Biden_meets_with_Prime_Minister_Benjamin_Netanyahu.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/The_White_House_-_54437645928.jpg/250px-The_White_House_-_54437645928.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_White_House_-_54437645928.jpg)

* US Air Force aircraft [carrying ammunition to Israel](/wiki/United_States_support_for_Israel_in_the_Gaza_war "United States support for Israel in the Gaza war") on October 15, 2023
* US President [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") on a support visit to Israeli Prime Minister [Benjamin Netanyahu](/wiki/Benjamin_Netanyahu "Benjamin Netanyahu") in Tel Aviv on October 18, 2023
* US President [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") receiving Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu at the White House on April 7, 2025.

Since 7 October, [the United States has aided Israel](/wiki/United_States_support_for_Israel_in_the_Gaza_war "United States support for Israel in the Gaza war") in pursuing its war goals in Gaza as well as attempting to deter Hamas's allies in the region and to prevent them from expanding the scope of confrontation with Israel in the Middle East.[[595]](#cite_note-Chamas2024-609): 67 The extent of American support for Israel has surpassed its involvement in all previous Israeli wars, leading [Gilbert Achcar](/wiki/Gilbert_Achcar "Gilbert Achcar") to call it 'the first US-Israeli joint war'.[[596]](#cite_note-610) Similarly, French historian Jean-Pierre Filiu argued that the extent of American support and involvement for Israel in its war against Palestine makes it a co-belligerent.[[597]](#cite_note-611) The United States has funded 70% of the total costs of the Gaza war since October 2023. The true level of US investment is higher, as this figure does not include its aid to Egypt and Jordan, which is intended to maintain their peace treaties with Israel.[[598]](#cite_note-612) Israel was also allowed to supply itself from American weapon stockpiles located in Israel.[[595]](#cite_note-Chamas2024-609): 70

Alongside substantial financial and diplomatic support, the US also intervened in the war directly. 100 American soldiers were deployed in combat to man a [THAAD](/wiki/THAAD "THAAD") anti-air battery.[[599]](#cite_note-telegraph20241015-613)[[600]](#cite_note-nytimes20241013-614) In addition, the US piloted drones over Gaza to provide intelligence to Israel,[[601]](#cite_note-615) aimed at locating hostages and Palestinian militant leaders in Gaza;[[602]](#cite_note-616) this also included information on Sinwar's location.[[603]](#cite_note-617) During the Biden administration, the US sent a Marine three-star general and other U.S. military officers to Israel to advise on its operations in Gaza.[[604]](#cite_note-618) In November 2023, US special forces and commandos were deployed to help Israel track down hostages held in Gaza.[[605]](#cite_note-619) On 18 March 2025, after Israel's surprise attack on the Gaza Strip, Israeli government spokesman [David Mencer](/w/index.php?title=David_Mencer&action=edit&redlink=1 "David Mencer (page does not exist)") stated that the operation had been "fully coordinated with Washington" and thanked the [Trump administration](/wiki/Second_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "Second presidency of Donald Trump") "for their unyielding support for Israel".[[606]](#cite_note-620) On 6 April, a second THAAD system was deployed to Israel by the US.[[607]](#cite_note-621)

During negotiations for an end to the war, American officials said there were plans to send 200 US soldiers to Israel to oversee the implementation of the ceasefire, though they said they would not enter Gaza, but instead Central Command will establish a "civil-military coordination center".[[608]](#cite_note-622)

### Diplomatic impact

Main articles: [Diplomatic impact of the Gaza war](/wiki/Diplomatic_impact_of_the_Gaza_war "Diplomatic impact of the Gaza war") and [International reactions to the Gaza war](/wiki/International_reactions_to_the_Gaza_war "International reactions to the Gaza war")

Further information: [Arms embargoes on Israel since 2023](/wiki/Arms_embargoes_on_Israel_since_2023 "Arms embargoes on Israel since 2023"), [United States support for Israel in the Gaza war](/wiki/United_States_support_for_Israel_in_the_Gaza_war "United States support for Israel in the Gaza war"), [United Kingdom and the Gaza war](/wiki/United_Kingdom_and_the_Gaza_war "United Kingdom and the Gaza war"), [European Union reactions to the Gaza war](/wiki/European_Union_reactions_to_the_Gaza_war "European Union reactions to the Gaza war"), [Gaza war protests](/wiki/Gaza_war_protests "Gaza war protests"), [Violent incidents in reaction to the Gaza war](/wiki/Violent_incidents_in_reaction_to_the_Gaza_war "Violent incidents in reaction to the Gaza war"), and [Calls for a ceasefire during the Gaza war](/wiki/Calls_for_a_ceasefire_during_the_Gaza_war "Calls for a ceasefire during the Gaza war")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Secretary_Blinken_Meets_with_Foreign_Ministers_of_the_Gulf_Cooperation_Council_Member_States_-_53689212351.jpg/250px-Secretary_Blinken_Meets_with_Foreign_Ministers_of_the_Gulf_Cooperation_Council_Member_States_-_53689212351.jpg)](/wiki/File:Secretary_Blinken_Meets_with_Foreign_Ministers_of_the_Gulf_Cooperation_Council_Member_States_-_53689212351.jpg)

US Secretary of State [Antony Blinken](/wiki/Antony_Blinken "Antony Blinken") and foreign ministers of the [Gulf Cooperation Council](/wiki/Gulf_Cooperation_Council "Gulf Cooperation Council") member states in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, 19 April 2024

The war sparked a [diplomatic crisis](/wiki/Diplomatic_crisis "Diplomatic crisis") and affected the momentum of regional relations.[[609]](#cite_note-623) Negotiations have focused on the possibility of a ceasefire, with the US, [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") and [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar "Qatar") serving as mediators.[[610]](#cite_note-624)[[611]](#cite_note-625) The [United Nations Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council") passed [resolution 2728](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_Resolution_2728 "United Nations Security Council Resolution 2728") in March 2024, calling for an immediate ceasefire,[[253]](#cite_note-aljazeera20240325-263)[[612]](#cite_note-626) and [resolution 2735](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_Resolution_2735 "United Nations Security Council Resolution 2735") in June 2024, calling for acceptance of the [three-phase ceasefire proposal](/wiki/January_2025_Gaza_war_ceasefire "January 2025 Gaza war ceasefire").[[613]](#cite_note-627)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Vice_President_Harris_met_with_President_Herzog_of_Israel_during_the_MSC_2024.jpg/250px-Vice_President_Harris_met_with_President_Herzog_of_Israel_during_the_MSC_2024.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vice_President_Harris_met_with_President_Herzog_of_Israel_during_the_MSC_2024.jpg)

US Vice President [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris") with Israeli President [Isaac Herzog](/wiki/Isaac_Herzog "Isaac Herzog") at the [60th Munich Security Conference](/wiki/60th_Munich_Security_Conference "60th Munich Security Conference") in Germany, February 2024

Significant geopolitical divisions emerged during the war. Israel's traditional [Western](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") allies provided diplomatic backing and weaponry to Israel;[[614]](#cite_note-Busari-2024-628) including the United States,[[615]](#cite_note-629) United Kingdom,[[616]](#cite_note-Chuter-2023-630) and Germany.[[617]](#cite_note-631) At least 44 nations denounced Hamas and explicitly condemned its conduct on 7 October as terrorism, including a joint statement by the US, UK, France, Italy, and Germany.[[618]](#cite_note-WI-Waldo-632)

Several European nations have been less supportive of Israel's actions, most notably [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway"), and [Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") who formally [recognized the State of Palestine](/wiki/International_recognition_of_Palestine "International recognition of Palestine") in a coordinated move in June 2024.[[619]](#cite_note-633) Spain and Ireland have also supported [South Africa's genocide case against Israel](/wiki/South_Africa%27s_genocide_case_against_Israel "South Africa's genocide case against Israel").[[620]](#cite_note-634)[[621]](#cite_note-635) This has led to retaliatory action by Israel, who recalled its ambassadors to all three countries and later announced that it would be closing its embassy in Dublin.[[622]](#cite_note-636)[[623]](#cite_note-637)[[624]](#cite_note-638)

The [Islamic world](/wiki/Islamic_world "Islamic world") and much of the [Global South](/wiki/Global_South "Global South") denounced the actions of Israel and its allies, criticizing the "moral authority of the West" and alleging that it holds [double standards](/wiki/Double_standard "Double standard") surrounding [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights"),[[614]](#cite_note-Busari-2024-628)[[625]](#cite_note-Phillips-2023-639) comparing the alleged [double standards](/wiki/Double_standards "Double standards") of condemning [Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russia%27s_2022_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine") while supporting Israel's occupation of Palestine.[[626]](#cite_note-640) At least nine countries withdrew their ambassadors or cut diplomatic ties with Israel.[[627]](#cite_note-641) Global public opinion of Israel dropped during the war; a Morning Consult poll published in January 2024 indicated that the United States was the only remaining wealthy country in which Israel had net positive approval.[[628]](#cite_note-d904-642) The Israeli government's response prompted international [protests](/wiki/Gaza_war_protests "Gaza war protests"), arrests, and [harassment](/wiki/Violent_incidents_in_reaction_to_the_Gaza_war "Violent incidents in reaction to the Gaza war").[[629]](#cite_note-643)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Stop_the_Genocide_Now%21_Save_the_Children_of_Gaza%21_Demonstration_%2853292404176%29.jpg/250px-Stop_the_Genocide_Now%21_Save_the_Children_of_Gaza%21_Demonstration_%2853292404176%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stop_the_Genocide_Now!_Save_the_Children_of_Gaza!_Demonstration_(53292404176).jpg)

Pro-Palestinian protest in [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki"), Finland, 28 October 2023

On 21 September 2025, the United Kingdom, Australia, Canada, and Portugal formally [recognised](/wiki/International_recognition_of_Palestine "International recognition of Palestine") a Palestinian state.[[630]](#cite_note-644) France, Luxembourg, Monaco, Malta, and others did so the following day.[[631]](#cite_note-645)

## Peace plan

Main article: [Gaza peace plan](/wiki/Gaza_peace_plan "Gaza peace plan")

See also: [Post-war plans for the Gaza Strip](/wiki/Post-war_plans_for_the_Gaza_Strip "Post-war plans for the Gaza Strip"), [Yellow Line (Gaza)](/wiki/Yellow_Line_(Gaza) "Yellow Line (Gaza)"), [Gaza International Transitional Authority](/wiki/Gaza_International_Transitional_Authority "Gaza International Transitional Authority"), [Board of Peace](/wiki/Board_of_Peace "Board of Peace"), and [International Stabilization Force](/wiki/International_Stabilization_Force "International Stabilization Force")

Following the US-backed ceasefire proposal's implementation in October 2025, a broader peace plan has been advanced by the Trump administration. Following the prisoner exchanges that took place in mid-October, the plan calls for the division of Gaza into an Israeli-controlled outer zone and a coastal region to be occupied by a US-led international force before power is transferred to Palestinian technocrats under the supervision of a "Board of Peace" chaired by Trump.[[632]](#cite_note-mondediplonovember2025-646) The [United Nations Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council") has authorized the [International Stabilization Force](/wiki/International_Stabilization_Force "International Stabilization Force") (ISF) and the [Board of Peace](/wiki/Board_of_Peace "Board of Peace") to operate in Gaza until 2027.[[633]](#cite_note-647) The plan calls for the disarmament of Hamas and demilitarization of the Gaza Strip,[[634]](#cite_note-648)[[635]](#cite_note-649)[[401]](#cite_note-pbs20250930-411)[[636]](#cite_note-650) and the creation of a [special economic zone](/wiki/Special_economic_zone "Special economic zone"). The reconstruction of Gaza is estimated to cost $70 billion, and had "no modern comparison" according to a Brookings Institution researcher.[[637]](#cite_note-651) The details of the proposal have been contentious, with Hamas insisting on Palestinian governance being "based on Palestinian national consensus"[[632]](#cite_note-mondediplonovember2025-646) and rejecting disarmament among other US demands.[[638]](#cite_note-652)[[639]](#cite_note-653) The ceasefire has been violated by both sides.[[640]](#cite_note-654)[[641]](#cite_note-655) According to *[The New Arab](/wiki/The_New_Arab "The New Arab")*, Israeli ceasefire violations have complicated the broader peace plan's implementation,[[642]](#cite_note-656) with continued Israeli strikes in Gaza killing more than 370 Palestinians since the start of the ceasefire.[[643]](#cite_note-aljazeera20251207-657)

In December 2025, IDF Chief of Staff Eyal Zamir said that the Yellow Line would be Israel's new border line in the Gaza Strip,[[644]](#cite_note-658) despite the Gaza peace plan's stipulation that Israel will not occupy or annex Gaza.[[645]](#cite_note-659) Zamir said that Israel would remain in operational control over territory beyond the Yellow Line, which amounts to 53% of the Gaza Strip.[[643]](#cite_note-aljazeera20251207-657) Israel has moved the Yellow Line further into Gaza[[646]](#cite_note-660)[[647]](#cite_note-661) and applies a shoot-to-kill policy toward anyone approaching or crossing the line.[[648]](#cite_note-662)[[649]](#cite_note-663)[[650]](#cite_note-664) Within parts of Gaza, the Yellow Line is not clearly marked or delineated.[[651]](#cite_note-665) The Trump administration has said that it plans to build prefabricated structures on Israel's side of the Yellow Line; the plan was met with skepticism from Israeli officials and Arab diplomats. Arab diplomats have expressed reluctance to deploy ISF troops on the Palestinian side of the Yellow Line, suggesting that it would be better to deploy the ISF in lieu of Israeli soldiers first.[[652]](#cite_note-666) An analysis by [Forensic Architecture](/wiki/Forensic_Architecture "Forensic Architecture") and [Drop Site News](/wiki/Drop_Site_News "Drop Site News") showed that Israel had built 13 new outposts in Gaza since the start of the ceasefire. Palestinian property in eastern Khan Younis was systematically destroyed in areas where new military infrastructure was built.[[653]](#cite_note-667) On 24 December, Defense Minister Israel Katz suggested that Israel would build settlements in Gaza, but later walked back his comments.[[654]](#cite_note-668) In a joint statement on Israeli recognition of Somaliland, several Arab and Muslim states including Saudi Arabia, Egypt, Jordan and Turkey expressed concern that Israel intended to expel the Palestinian population in Gaza and resettle Palestinians in East Africa.[[655]](#cite_note-669)

## Public approval

See also: [Gaza war protests in Israel](/wiki/Gaza_war_protests_in_Israel "Gaza war protests in Israel")

There was broad support in Israeli society for military operations in Gaza.[[656]](#cite_note-670)[[657]](#cite_note-671) A public opinion poll conducted in December 2023 by the [Israel Democracy Institute](/wiki/Israel_Democracy_Institute "Israel Democracy Institute") found that 87% of Jewish Israelis supported the war in Gaza.[[658]](#cite_note-672) In another Israel Democracy Institute survey of 510 Israeli citizens in early February 2024, 68% of respondents supported preventing all international aid from entering Gaza.[[659]](#cite_note-673) A poll commissioned by [Penn State University](/wiki/Penn_State_University "Penn State University") and conducted in March 2025 among a representative sample of 1,005 Israeli Jews found that 82% supported the forced expulsion of Gaza residents. Additionally, 47% responded affirmatively to the question: "When conquering an enemy city, should the IDF act like the Israelites led by Joshua when they [conquered Jericho](/wiki/Fall_of_Jericho "Fall of Jericho"), that is, kill all its inhabitants?"[[660]](#cite_note-674)[[661]](#cite_note-675)

Throughout the war, [multiple protests](/wiki/Gaza_war_protests_in_Israel "Gaza war protests in Israel") have been held in Israel. As of September 2025 weekly protests took place in Tel Aviv and other locations in Israel with thousands of protestors calling for an end to the war and a hostage release deal.[[662]](#cite_note-676) The Knesset's law criminalizing "terrorist materials" consumption drew criticism.[[663]](#cite_note-677)

A December 2023 poll by PCPSR found that 72% of respondents (52% of Gazans and 85% of West Bank residents) approved of the October 7 attacks.[[664]](#cite_note-678) A 2024 follow-up poll found that two-thirds of respondents continued to approve of the attacks.[[665]](#cite_note-stern-679) About 90% of respondents did not believe that Hamas committed atrocities (killing women and children, sexual violence) during the October 7 attacks, seeing this as enemy propaganda.[[665]](#cite_note-stern-679) Half of respondents in Gaza expected Hamas to win the war, while a quarter expected Israel to win.[[665]](#cite_note-stern-679)

## Media coverage

Main article: [Media coverage of the Gaza war](/wiki/Media_coverage_of_the_Gaza_war "Media coverage of the Gaza war")

In reporting on the conflict, foreign media have limited access to Gaza and only in the presence of Israeli soldiers. *[Vox](/wiki/Vox_(website) "Vox (website)")* reported that the news organizations "have to submit all materials and footage to the IDF for review before publication".[[666]](#cite_note-Narea-2023-680) The conflict has also seen large numbers of journalists wounded or killed. The [International Federation of Journalists](/wiki/International_Federation_of_Journalists "International Federation of Journalists") stated that as many journalists were killed in the first two months of the Gaza war as in the two decades of the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War").[[667]](#cite_note-Price-2023-681) [Reporters Without Borders](/wiki/Reporters_Without_Borders "Reporters Without Borders") filed a complaint with the [International Criminal Court](/wiki/International_Criminal_Court "International Criminal Court") under the [Rome Statute](/wiki/Rome_Statute "Rome Statute"), accusing Israel of committing war crimes against eight journalists.[[668]](#cite_note-rsf20231101-682)[[666]](#cite_note-Narea-2023-680) It also lodged a complaint against Hamas for the killing of a reporter covering the 7 October attack.[[668]](#cite_note-rsf20231101-682) The [Committee to Protect Journalists](/wiki/Committee_to_Protect_Journalists "Committee to Protect Journalists") accused Israel of targeting journalists reporting from Gaza and their families.[[669]](#cite_note-683)
